content.text:
<analysis>
a. Event type: Pull request opened - I'm reviewing a PR that salvages an AI review of P. putida OprF gene
b. This is a request for code review feedback - the user wants me to review the PR for code quality, bugs, performance, security, and test coverage
c. Key information:
   - PR adds 10 files (3820 additions, 0 deletions)
   - Contains gene review data: ai-review.yaml, deep-research, GOA data, UniProt data, publications
   - Copilot reviewer has already identified issues with file paths, extraction artifacts, and line endings
   - Files include publication extracts, InterPro/PANTHER metadata, and structured gene review
d. Main tasks: Review all added files for quality, identify issues beyond what Copilot found, check adherence to repository guidelines
e. Plan: Read CLAUDE.md for repo guidelines, examine each file type systematically, validate YAML structure, check file organization and naming conventions
f. I should be able to complete this review fully as it's primarily file analysis and feedback
</analysis>

I'll review this pull request focusing on code quality, potential issues, and adherence to repository standards. Let me start by examining the repository guidelines and then systematically review the added files.

input.body: ### PR Review: P. putida OprF Gene Review Reviewing this salvaged gene review for code quality, best practices, and potential issues. #### Todo List: - [ ] Read repository guidelines from CLAUDE.md - [ ] Review gene review YAML structure and validation - [ ] Examine publication files for quality issues - [ ] Check InterPro/PANTHER metadata files - [ ] Validate file organization and naming conventions - [ ] Check for security concerns - [ ] Assess performance implications - [ ] Provide recommendations --- [View job run](https://github.com/ai4curation/ai-gene-review/actions/runs/23419546739)

content.content:
1→# AI instructions for ai-gene-review
     2→
     3→## Project Overview
     4→
     5→This project is for managing AI reviews of gene annotations. The overall goal is to both
     6→review existing GO annotations using strict defined criteria, and to summarize the resulting
     7→high quality annotations.
     8→
     9→## Repository layout
    10→
    11→src/
    12→  ai_gene_review/
    13→    schema/
    14→      gene_review.yaml <-- linkml schema for review docs
    15→genes/
    16→  human/
    17→    CFAP300/
    18→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- keep your notes here
    19→      CFAP300-ai-review.yaml <-- the output of your AI review
    20→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- your own notes, edit this, use it like a journal and keep appending.
    21→      CFAP300-uniprot.txt <-- the uniprot record downloaded from the UniProt website (do not edit this file)
    22→      CFAP300-goa.tsv <-- the GO annotation downloaded from the QuickGO API (do not edit this file, no need to view it)
    23→      CFAP300-deep-research.md <-- the deep research for the gene review (do not edit this file, it is automatically generated for you)
    24→      CFAP300-citations.md <-- the citations for the gene review (do not edit this file)
    25→      CFAP300-bioinformatics/ <-- the folder for any bioinformatics analyses (create this if necessary)
    26→      CFAP300-predictions-review.yaml <-- review of computational predictions NOT in GOA (optional)
    27→    <other genes>/
    28→      ...
    29→  worm/
    30→    lrx-1/
    31→  SCHPO/  <-- other orgs use uniprot species codes
    32→      ...
    33→publications/
    34→  PMID_123456.md <-- cached publication text (this should be available for you, DO NOT EDIT)
    35→reactome/
    36→  R-HSA-NNNNNN.md <- cached reactome entry
    37→
    38→You can regenerate the derived files by running commands like:
    39→
    40→```
    41→just fetch-gene human CFAP300 
    42→```
    43→
    44→This makes the uniprot, goa, and publications files. It will also create a stub for the ai-review.yaml if it doesn't exist.
    45→
    46→After making edits to the ai-review.yaml file, you can validate the file by running:
    47→
    48→```
    49→just validate human CFAP300
    50→```
    51→
    52→This ensures that the yaml file is consistent with the schema in `src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml`, and also
    53→that it implements best practices.
    54→
    55→To validate all files, run:
    56→
    57→```
    58→just validate-all
    59→```
    60→
    61→files will be written to reports/validation-all.tsv 
    62→
    63→* All gene symbols follow the standard nomenclature for that species. For human, should be HGNC symbols.
    64→
    65→## Gene Review Guidelines
    66→
    67→Your job is to review existing annotations for a gene, following GO guidelines, and synthesize these with what is known in the literature,
    68→and what can be inferred bioinformatically. The general structure of the review is as follows:
    69→
    70→```yaml
    71→id: <uniprot id>
    72→gene_symbol: <HGNC symbol>
    73→taxon:
    74→  id: <NCBI taxon id>
    75→  label: <taxon label>
    76→description: <your own summary of the gene>
    77→references:
    78→  <all relevant reference, including both literature and bioinformatics sources; includes all but not limited to those used in existing_annotations>
    79→existing_annotations:
    80→  <review of existing annotations, using the GO guidelines; should include all annotations from goa.csv>
    81→core_functions:
    82→  <your own summary of the core functions of the gene>
    83→proposed_new_terms:
    84→  <suggestions for new GO terms>
    85→suggested_questions:
    86→  <suggested questions for experts>
    87→suggested_experiments:
    88→  <suggested experiments to elucidate gene function>
    89→```
    90→
    91→See the schema and existing files for more details.
    92→
    93→When creating a new review, always make sure files are in place:
    94→
    95→`just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`
    96→
    97→This creates the uniprot, goa files, and a stub for the gene review.
    98→
    99→The next thing is for you to do deep research on that gene and make a GENE-notes.md file, with citations inline (e.g [PMID:12345 title, "<supporting text>"]). In some cases this will have been done ahead of time for you.
   100→
   101→## Reviewing existing annotations
   102→
   103→You should start with the ai-review.yaml file, where stubs are created for you (from `just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`). Any PMID cited should be in the publications folder, e.g. `publications/PMID_123456.md`. Review these.
   104→
   105→A deep research file should already be present in the gene folder, e.g. `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-deep-research-PROVIDER.md` (where PROVIDER is openai, falcon, perplexity, etc.). This should contain a summary of the literature, and any other information you found useful for the review. There may be irrelevant information in the citations, that is OK. If this is not present, you can generate one using:
   106→
   107→```bash
   108→just deep-research SPECIES GENE --provider perplexity
   109→```
   110→
   111→This will create a file like `GENE-deep-research-perplexity.md`. You can also make your own notes in `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-notes.md`.
   112→
   113→Be aware that many GO terms are over-annotations. You should also not take existing annotations as gospel, whether experimental or bioinformatic.
   114→always use a holistic synthesized picture of the gene function that you have obtained from your research.
   115→
   116→```yaml
   117→ActionEnum:
   118→    permissible_values:
   119→      ACCEPT:
   120→        description: Accept the existing annotation as-is, no modifications, and retain as representing the core function of the gene
   121→      KEEP_AS_NON_CORE:
   122→        description: Keep the existing annotation as-is, but mark it as non-core. For pleiotropic genes, this may be the developmental
   123→          processes, or other processes that are not the core function of the gene.
   124→      REMOVE:
   125→        description: Remove the existing annotation, as it is unlikely to be correct based on combined evidence
   126→      MODIFY:
   127→        description: The essence of the annotation is sound, but there are better terms to use (use in combination with proposed_replacement_terms).
   128→          if the term is too general, then MODIFY should be used, with a proposed replacement term for the correct specific function.
   129→          sometimes terms can also be overly specific and contorted, so in some cases you might want to generalize
   130→      MARK_AS_OVER_ANNOTATED:
   131→        description: The term is not entirely wrong, but likely represents an over-annotation of the gene
   132→      UNDECIDED:
   133→        description: The annotation is not clear, and the reviewer is not sure what to do with it. ALWAYS USE THIS IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO ACCESS
   134→          RELEVANT PUBLICATIONS
   135→      NEW:
   136→        
   137→```      
   138→
   139→## Tools
   140→
   141→Use the OLS MCP to find relevant ontology terms, if the terms you need are not in existing_annotations.
   142→
   143→## Curation guidelines
   144→
   145→Avoid the term `protein binding`, this doesn't tell us anything about the actual function. Instead find a more
   146→informative MF term (e.g for adapter function)
   147→
   148→## Isoform and Negation Tracking
   149→
   150→The system tracks isoform-specific GO annotations and NOT (negated) annotations:
   151→
   152→```yaml
   153→existing_annotations:
   154→# Isoform-specific annotation
   155→- term:
   156→    id: GO:0045892
   157→    label: negative regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   158→  evidence_type: IDA
   159→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   160→  isoform: P19544-1  # UniProt isoform ID - only present for isoform-specific annotations
   161→  review:
   162→    summary: ...
   163→
   164→# NOT annotation (negated)
   165→- term:
   166→    id: GO:0045893
   167→    label: positive regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   168→  evidence_type: IDA
   169→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   170→  negated: true  # This is a NOT annotation
   171→  isoform: P19544-1
   172→  review:
   173→    summary: WT1 does NOT activate transcription...
   174→```
   175→
   176→- **`isoform`**: UniProt isoform ID (e.g., `P19544-1`) - indicates what isoform was tested
   177→- **`negated`**: Set to `true` for NOT annotations (e.g., `NOT|involved_in` qualifier in GOA)
   178→
   179→**Important**: Just because an annotation was made on a specific isoform doesn't mean the function is isoform-specific.
   180→The `isoform` field indicates what was tested, not necessarily what is unique to that isoform.
   181→
   182→To backfill isoform info on existing reviews:
   183→```bash
   184→just backfill-isoforms human WT1
   185→just backfill-isoforms-organism human  # all genes in organism
   186→```
   187→
   188→See `docs/isoform_tracking.md` for full documentation and `projects/ISOFORMS.md` for genes with notable isoform-specific functions.
   189→
   190→## Bioinformatics analyses
   191→
   192→In some cases, it may be useful to do additional bioinformatics analyses. To validate gene function. Here are some guidelines:
   193→
   194→For a gene-specific analysis, the folder is `<genes/specues/GENE>-bioinformatics/`. You should make this be reproducible. You can do a `uv init`
   195→here and `uv add` for any packages you need.
   196→
   197→Never hardcode the results of the script, it's OK for a script to return inconclusive results. Never ever make up results.
   198→
   199→**IMPORTANT**: DO NOT write scripts that just tell users to go to a website. If you cannot programmatically call a tool (e.g., requires API keys, browser interaction, or authentication you don't have), then:
   200→1. Be honest about this limitation upfront
   201→2. Write scripts that can parse/process the output from the tool if the user runs it manually
   202→3. Never write misleading code that pretends to do something it doesn't actually do
   203→
   204→You can summarize the results of the bioinformatics analyses in the `<GENE>-bioinformatics/RESULTS.md` file. This can be referenced as any other reference
   205→as e.g `file:<SPECIES>/<GENE>/bioinformatics/RESULTS.md`.
   206→
   207→## Reviewing Computational Predictions
   208→
   209→The `GENE-predictions-review.yaml` file reviews computational/ML predictions that are NOT
   210→in the curated GOA/UniProt annotations. This is separate from `existing_annotations` in the
   211→main review file.
   212→
   213→Use the `PredictionReview` class (validated with `-C PredictionReview`):
   214→
   215→```bash
   216→uv run linkml-validate -s src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml -C PredictionReview genes/ECOLI/yciO/yciO-predictions-review.yaml
   217→```
   218→
   219→### Prediction sources
   220→
   221→Track predictions from: DeepECTF, PANTHER/IBA, InterPro2GO, CLEAN, GloEC, MAPred, or any
   222→other computational method that produces GO or EC predictions.
   223→
   224→### Assessment categories (from de Crécy-Lagard et al. 2025, PMID:40703034)
   225→
   226→| Code | CS | Description |
   227→|------|-----|-------------|
   228→| COR  | 2   | Correct novel prediction |
   229→| CNN  | 2   | Correct but Not Novel (already in training data) |
   230→| LSP  | 2   | Less Precise than existing annotation |
   231→| UNC  | 1   | Uncertain - cannot validate or refute |
   232→| PLI  | 0   | Paralog Incorrect - wrong paralog subfamily |
   233→| NPI  | 0   | Nonparalog Incorrect - refuted by evidence |
   234→| REP  | 0   | Repetition - frequency-biased prediction |
   235→
   236→### Error types
   237→
   238→- `PARALOG_OVERANNOTATION` - Wrong paralog subfamily (Type 6)
   239→- `PATHWAY_CONTEXT_IGNORED` - Predicted pathway absent from organism
   240→- `FREQUENCY_BIAS` - Model defaults to high-frequency training labels
   241→- `IN_VITRO_NOT_IN_VIVO` - In vitro activity doesn't match biological function
   242→- `TRAINING_DATA_CONTAMINATION` - Prediction already in training data
   243→- See schema for full list
   244→
   245→## General guidelines
   246→
   247→* NEVER guess identifiers for terms, genes, publications. Always use the relevant tools or MCPS, or look them up in derived files.
   248→* For files `<GENE>-notes.md`, use literature deep search, and always record provenance for assertions, e.g `[PMID:12345 "<supporting text>"]`
   249→
   250→## Support code
   251→
   252→There is also support code in `src/ai_gene_review`, see the CLAUDE.md file in that directory for more details
   253→on best practices for working with the code.
   254→
   255→
   256→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer high-level questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

input.body: ### PR Review: P. putida OprF Gene Review Reviewing this salvaged gene review for code quality, best practices, and potential issues. #### Todo List: - [x] Read repository guidelines from CLAUDE.md - [ ] Review gene review YAML structure and validation - [ ] Examine publication files for quality issues - [ ] Check InterPro/PANTHER metadata files - [ ] Validate file organization and naming conventions - [ ] Check for security concerns - [ ] Assess performance implications - [ ] Provide recommendations --- [View job run](https://github.com/ai4curation/ai-gene-review/actions/runs/23419546739)

content.content:
1→id: Q88L46
     2→gene_symbol: oprF
     3→product_type: PROTEIN
     4→status: DRAFT
     5→taxon:
     6→  id: NCBITaxon:160488
     7→  label: Pseudomonas putida (strain ATCC 47054 / DSM 6125 / CFBP 8728 / NCIMB 11950
     8→    / KT2440)
     9→description: OprF is the major OmpA/OprF-family outer membrane protein of Pseudomonas
    10→  putida KT2440. The protein has a signal peptide, an N-terminal outer-membrane beta-barrel
    11→  porin region, and a C-terminal OmpA-like periplasmic domain. Combined evidence from
    12→  KT2440 studies and canonical OprF literature supports a core role in outer membrane
    13→  permeability and envelope integrity, while the current InterPro-derived calcium-binding
    14→  prediction lacks direct biochemical support.
    15→existing_annotations:
    16→- term:
    17→    id: GO:0005509
    18→    label: calcium ion binding
    19→  evidence_type: IEA
    20→  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000002
    21→  review:
    22→    summary: This InterPro-derived annotation is weak for OprF. The established biology
    23→      of OprF in KT2440 and canonical homologs is outer-membrane localization, porin
    24→      activity, and envelope integrity rather than calcium binding.
    25→    action: REMOVE
    26→    reason: I found no direct biochemical evidence that KT2440 OprF binds calcium.
    27→      The annotation appears to come from a low-specificity domain match in the acidic
    28→      C-terminal region and does not reflect the conserved core function of the OprF
    29→      family.
    30→    supported_by:
    31→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/oprF/oprF-deep-research-falcon.md
    32→      supporting_text: 'The InterPro-derived calcium ion binding annotation is weak:
    33→        I found no direct biochemical evidence that KT2440 OprF binds calcium, and
    34→        the predicted thrombospondin type 3 repeat appears non-core relative to the
    35→        conserved porin/OmpA architecture.'
    36→      reference_section_type: RESULTS
    37→- term:
    38→    id: GO:0009279
    39→    label: cell outer membrane
    40→  evidence_type: IEA
    41→  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000120
    42→  review:
    43→    summary: This is the correct specific localization for OprF. Both UniProt and
    44→      KT2440 literature identify OprF as the major outer membrane porin.
    45→    action: ACCEPT
    46→    reason: Cell outer membrane localization is directly consistent with the protein
    47→      architecture, UniProt subcellular annotation, and KT2440 studies manipulating
    48→      OprF as a major outer membrane porin.
    49→    supported_by:
    50→    - reference_id: PMID:32211393
    51→      supporting_text: Then the major outer membrane porin OprF and the specific porin
    52→        OprE were overproduced during PHA producing conditions on decanoate-grown
    53→        cells.
    54→      reference_section_type: ABSTRACT
    55→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/oprF/oprF-deep-research-falcon.md
    56→      supporting_text: The current evidence supports outer-membrane localization and
    57→        porin activity as core features of KT2440 OprF.
    58→      reference_section_type: RESULTS
    59→- term:
    60→    id: GO:0016020
    61→    label: membrane
    62→  evidence_type: IEA
    63→  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000120
    64→  review:
    65→    summary: This annotation is broadly correct but less informative than the coexisting
    66→      cell outer membrane annotation.
    67→    action: KEEP_AS_NON_CORE
    68→    reason: OprF is certainly a membrane protein, but the more specific and biologically
    69→      useful localization is GO:0009279 cell outer membrane.
    70→    supported_by:
    71→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/oprF/oprF-deep-research-falcon.md
    72→      supporting_text: The current evidence supports outer-membrane localization and
    73→        porin activity as core features of KT2440 OprF.
    74→      reference_section_type: RESULTS
    75→- term:
    76→    id: GO:0015288
    77→    label: porin activity
    78→  evidence_type: TAS
    79→  review:
    80→    summary: OprF should carry a core porin activity annotation even though it is
    81→      absent from the current QuickGO snapshot. KT2440 papers call it the major outer
    82→      membrane porin, and canonical OprF homologs have direct channel-forming evidence.
    83→    action: NEW
    84→    reason: This term captures the core molecular function of the OprF family more
    85→      precisely than broad membrane localization terms. The addition is conservative
    86→      because it is supported by direct author statements in KT2440 and by classic
    87→      OprF pore-formation work in the canonical homolog.
    88→    supported_by:
    89→    - reference_id: PMID:32211393
    90→      supporting_text: Then the major outer membrane porin OprF and the specific porin
    91→        OprE were overproduced during PHA producing conditions on decanoate-grown
    92→        cells.
    93→      reference_section_type: ABSTRACT
    94→    - reference_id: PMID:16595653
    95→      supporting_text: The major nonspecific porin of Pseudomonas aeruginosa, OprF,
    96→        produces a large channel yet allows only a slow diffusion of various solutes.
    97→      reference_section_type: ABSTRACT
    98→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/oprF/oprF-deep-research-falcon.md
    99→      supporting_text: Canonical OprF homologs are nonspecific porins; in P. aeruginosa,
   100→        purified OprF produces large channels, although only a minority of molecules
   101→        adopt the open pore conformation.
   102→      reference_section_type: RESULTS
   103→references:
   104→- id: GO_REF:0000002
   105→  title: Gene Ontology annotation through association of InterPro records with GO
   106→    terms
   107→  findings: []
   108→- id: GO_REF:0000120
   109→  title: Combined Automated Annotation using Multiple IEA Methods
   110→  findings: []
   111→- id: PMID:32211393
   112→  title: Engineering the Osmotic State of Pseudomonas putida KT2440 for Efficient
   113→    Cell Disruption and Downstream Processing of Poly(3-Hydroxyalkanoates)
   114→  findings:
   115→  - statement: OprF is a major outer membrane porin in Pseudomonas putida KT2440
   116→    supporting_text: Then the major outer membrane porin OprF and the specific porin
   117→      OprE were overproduced during PHA producing conditions on decanoate-grown cells.
   118→    reference_section_type: ABSTRACT
   119→- id: PMID:40607986
   120→  title: Engineered Membrane Vesicle Production via oprF or oprI Deletion Has Distinct
   121→    Phenotypic Effects in Pseudomonas putida
   122→  findings:
   123→  - statement: Deletion of oprF increases vesiculation and membrane permeability
   124→    supporting_text: In contrast, deletion of oprF, relative to wild type, generated
   125→      up to a 4-fold increase in MVs but diminished growth, permeabilized membranes,
   126→      and increased cytosolic protein packaging.
   127→    reference_section_type: ABSTRACT
   128→- id: PMID:16595653
   129→  title: Pseudomonas aeruginosa porin OprF exists in two different conformations
   130→  findings:
   131→  - statement: Canonical OprF is a nonspecific porin that forms large channels
   132→    supporting_text: The major nonspecific porin of Pseudomonas aeruginosa, OprF,
   133→      produces a large channel yet allows only a slow diffusion of various solutes.
   134→    reference_section_type: ABSTRACT
   135→  - statement: Only a minority of OprF molecules adopt the open channel state
   136→    supporting_text: only about 5% of the OprF population produced open channels.
   137→    reference_section_type: ABSTRACT
   138→- id: PMID:8830709
   139→  title: Secondary structure of the outer membrane proteins OmpA of Escherichia coli
   140→    and OprF of Pseudomonas aeruginosa
   141→  findings: []
   142→- id: file:PSEPK/oprF/oprF-deep-research-falcon.md
   143→  title: Manual fallback deep research on oprF in Pseudomonas putida KT2440
   144→  findings:
   145→  - statement: OprF is the major outer membrane porin and supports envelope integrity
   146→    supporting_text: OprF is the major outer membrane porin of Pseudomonas putida
   147→      KT2440 and its loss causes membrane permeabilization and hypervesiculation.
   148→    reference_section_type: RESULTS
   149→  - statement: OprF has canonical OprF/OmpA family architecture
   150→    supporting_text: The Q88L46 UniProt entry assigns a 24-aa signal peptide, an N-terminal
   151→      outer membrane beta-barrel region, and a C-terminal OmpA-like domain (residues
   152→      225-343).
   153→    reference_section_type: RESULTS
   154→  - statement: The calcium ion binding annotation is weak and likely non-core
   155→    supporting_text: 'The InterPro-derived calcium ion binding annotation is weak:
   156→      I found no direct biochemical evidence that KT2440 OprF binds calcium, and the
   157→      predicted thrombospondin type 3 repeat appears non-core relative to the conserved
   158→      porin/OmpA architecture.'
   159→    reference_section_type: RESULTS
   160→core_functions:
   161→- molecular_function:
   162→    id: GO:0015288
   163→    label: porin activity
   164→  locations:
   165→  - id: GO:0009279
   166→    label: cell outer membrane
   167→  supported_by:
   168→  - reference_id: PMID:32211393
   169→    supporting_text: Then the major outer membrane porin OprF and the specific porin
   170→      OprE were overproduced during PHA producing conditions on decanoate-grown cells.
   171→    reference_section_type: ABSTRACT
   172→  - reference_id: PMID:16595653
   173→    supporting_text: The major nonspecific porin of Pseudomonas aeruginosa, OprF,
   174→      produces a large channel yet allows only a slow diffusion of various solutes.
   175→    reference_section_type: ABSTRACT
   176→  description: OprF is the major outer membrane porin of KT2440. The best-supported
   177→    core function is passive pore formation in the cell outer membrane, with a related
   178→    contribution to overall envelope integrity.
   179→suggested_questions:
   180→- question: Does KT2440 OprF adopt the same low-abundance open-channel conformation
   181→    described for the canonical Pseudomonas aeruginosa homolog?
   182→- question: Is the InterPro-derived calcium ion binding assignment a false positive,
   183→    or does the acidic C-terminal region bind calcium under any physiological condition?
   184→- question: How much of the KT2440 ΔoprF envelope phenotype reflects loss of pore
   185→    function versus loss of OmpA-like periplasmic tethering?
   186→suggested_experiments:
   187→- description: Reconstitute purified KT2440 OprF into liposomes or planar bilayers
   188→    and quantify conductance, selectivity, and open-state frequency.
   189→  experiment_type: electrophysiology
   190→- description: Complement a KT2440 ΔoprF strain with domain truncations or targeted
   191→    C-terminal mutants and measure membrane permeability and vesicle production.
   192→  experiment_type: genetics
   193→- description: Test direct calcium binding of purified full-length OprF or the isolated
   194→    C-terminal domain by ITC, DSF, or metal-overlay assays.
   195→  experiment_type: biochemistry
   196→

1→---
     2→pmid: '40607986'
     3→title: Engineered Membrane Vesicle Production via oprF or oprI Deletion Has Distinct
     4→  Phenotypic Effects in Pseudomonas putida.
     5→authors:
     6→- Wilkes RA
     7→- Miller TE
     8→- Waldbauer J
     9→- Zhou N
    10→- Zhang L
    11→- DiBiase BN
    12→- Kamat NP
    13→- Aristilde L
    14→- Beckham GT
    15→- Werner AZ
    16→journal: ACS Synth Biol
    17→year: '2025'
    18→full_text_available: true
    19→full_text_extraction_method: xml
    20→pmcid: PMC12281618
    21→doi: 10.1021/acssynbio.5c00171
    22→---
    23→
    24→# Engineered Membrane Vesicle Production via oprF or oprI Deletion Has Distinct Phenotypic Effects in Pseudomonas putida.
    25→**Authors:** Wilkes RA, Miller TE, Waldbauer J, Zhou N, Zhang L, DiBiase BN, Kamat NP, Aristilde L, Beckham GT, Werner AZ
    26→**Journal:** ACS Synth Biol (2025)
    27→**DOI:** [10.1021/acssynbio.5c00171](https://doi.org/10.1021/acssynbio.5c00171)
    28→**PMC:** [PMC12281618](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12281618/)
    29→
    30→## Abstract
    31→
    32→1. ACS Synth Biol. 2025 Jul 18;14(7):2739-2752. doi: 10.1021/acssynbio.5c00171. 
    33→Epub 2025 Jul 3.
    34→
    35→Engineered Membrane Vesicle Production via oprF or oprI Deletion Has Distinct 
    36→Phenotypic Effects in Pseudomonas putida.
    37→
    38→Wilkes RA(1), Miller TE(1), Waldbauer J(2), Zhou N(3), Zhang L(2), DiBiase 
    39→BN(4)(5), Kamat NP(3)(5), Aristilde L(3), Beckham GT(1), Werner AZ(1).
    40→
    41→Author information:
    42→(1)Renewable Resources and Enabling Sciences Center, National Renewable Energy 
    43→Laboratory, Golden, Colorado 80401-3393, United States.
    44→(2)Department of the Geophysical Sciences, University of Chicago, Chicago, 
    45→Illinois 60637-5418, United States.
    46→(3)Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, McCormick School of 
    47→Engineering and Applied Science, Northwestern University, Evanston, Illinois 
    48→60208-0001, United States.
    49→(4)Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Northwestern University, 
    50→Evanston, Illinois 60208-0001, United States.
    51→(5)Department of Biomedical Engineering, Northwestern University, Evanston, 
    52→Illinois 60208-0001, United States.
    53→
    54→Membrane vesicle (MV) production is a natural phenomenon in Gram-negative 
    55→bacteria and represents an emerging synthetic biology tool for the secretion of 
    56→biomolecules or bioproducts. Manipulation of membrane components has proven 
    57→successful in enhancing MV production. However, the impact of membrane 
    58→disruptions on strain fitness and protein composition warrants further 
    59→investigation for the use of MVs in industrial bioprocesses. Here, we identify 
    60→and characterize two genetic engineering strategies for inducing 
    61→hypervesiculation─deletion of genes for the outer membrane porin OprF or the 
    62→lipoprotein OprI─in the commonly used platform Pseudomonas putida KT2440. 
    63→Deletion of oprI generated up to a 1.5-fold increase in MVs, larger MVs with a 
    64→greater proportion of outer membrane proteins, and no significant impact on 
    65→strain fitness compared to wild type. In contrast, deletion of oprF, relative to 
    66→wild type, generated up to a 4-fold increase in MVs but diminished growth, 
    67→permeabilized membranes, and increased cytosolic protein packaging. Both 
    68→hypervesiculation phenotypes increased nontargeted and MV-targeted mNeonGreen 
    69→extracellular signal by up to 6-fold, demonstrating vesiculation as a mechanism 
    70→for protein secretion. Despite increased blebbing of MVs from gene deletions, 
    71→proteins involved in membrane biosynthesis were not elevated relative to wild 
    72→type. Overexpression of gpsA, which initiates glycerophospholipid biosynthesis, 
    73→in the ΔoprF background improved the membrane integrity by 37% and maintained MV 
    74→formation, highlighting the importance of membrane biosynthesis in restoring the 
    75→membrane in hypervesiculating strains. Together, this study provides genetic 
    76→engineering strategies with corresponding phenotypic outcomes toward providing a 
    77→synthetic biology toolset for MV deployment in P. putida.
    78→
    79→DOI: 10.1021/acssynbio.5c00171
    80→PMCID: PMC12281618
    81→PMID: 40607986 [Indexed for MEDLINE]
    82→
    83→## Full Text
    84→
    85→IntroductionGram-negative bacteria generate membrane
    86→vesicles (MVs) by blebbing
    87→portions of the membrane to create spherical lipid bilayers encircling
    88→cellular constituents, including proteins and nucleic acids.
    89→,
    90→ MVs have a myriad of proposed functions, such as removing toxic
    91→compounds or misfolded proteins, improving resistance to chemical
    92→stressors, scavenging of essential nutrients (i.e., carbon, nitrogen,
    93→or metals), exchanging genetic material, and defending against phages.
    94→−
    95→
    96→ Due to their role in diverse cellular functions and ability to encapsulate
    97→biological molecules, MVs have gained attention as versatile tools
    98→for bioproduction, biosensing, drug delivery, and vaccine development,
    99→among other applications.
   100→−

    10→  name:
    11→    name: Bacterial Outer Membrane Structural/Functional
    12→    short: Bact_OuterMem_StrucFunc
    13→  description:
    14→  - text: The protein family is involved in various functions related to the structural
    15→      integrity and function of the bacterial outer membrane. Members of the family
    16→      play roles in maintaining the position of the peptidoglycan cell wall within
    17→      the periplasm, acting as porins with low permeability for small solutes, and
    18→      participating in cell division processes. Some proteins within the family are
    19→      part of the Tol-Pal system, crucial for outer membrane invagination during cell
    20→      division and outer membrane integrity. Others are involved in bacterial motility,
    21→      forming part of the flagellar motor complex, or in antiviral defense systems.
    22→      Additionally, certain members function in cell adhesion, biofilm formation,
    23→      and resistance to environmental stress, including phage and colicin sensitivity.
    24→      The family also includes proteins that may have roles in transport, ammonia
    25→      secretion, and apoptosis induction in host cells.

content.content:
1→id: https://ai4curation.io/ai-gene-review
     2→name: gene_review
     3→description: Schema for gene curation
     4→  Top level entity is a GeneReview, which is about a single gene (and its equivalent swiss-prot entry).
     5→  It contains a high level summary of the gene, plus a review of all existing annotations.
     6→  It also contains a list of core functions, which are GO-CAM-like annotons describing the core evolved
     7→  functions of the gene.
     8→  
     9→
    10→prefixes:
    11→  linkml: https://w3id.org/linkml/
    12→  gene_review: https://w3id.org/ai4curation/gene_review/
    13→  xsd: http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#
    14→  rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#
    15→  rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#
    16→  owl: http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#
    17→  oa: http://www.w3.org/ns/oa#
    18→  skos: http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#
    19→  dcat: http://www.w3.org/ns/dcat#
    20→  dcterms: http://purl.org/dc/terms/
    21→  ECO: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ECO_
    22→  IAO: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/IAO_
    23→  SO: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/SO_
    24→
    25→imports:
    26→  - linkml:types
    27→
    28→default_prefix: gene_review
    29→default_range: string
    30→
    31→slots:
    32→  id:
    33→    identifier: true
    34→  label:
    35→    description: Human readable name of the entity
    36→    required: true
    37→    slot_uri: rdfs:label
    38→  gene_symbol:
    39→    range: string
    40→    description: Symbol of the gene
    41→    required: true
    42→  product_type:
    43→    range: ProductTypeEnum
    44→    description: Type of gene product (protein, ncRNA, etc.)
    45→    required: false  ## TODO: change to required now we have added this everywhere
    46→    comments:
    47→      - currently not required, assumed PROTEIN by default, but this may be explicit in future
    48→  title:
    49→    range: string
    50→    description: Title of the entity
